target.name: Harbinger
target.founder: John Harris, Phillip Weicker, Will Eberts

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $500M last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Transportation (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 2 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 4 same-sector peers exist in Transportation, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Harbinger is a Series C transportation company founded by John Harris, Phillip Weicker, and Will Eberts, currently valued at $500M post-money following $358M in total capital raised. The relevant comp universe consists of 8 private frontier-transportation firms including Rivian, The Boring Company, Einride, REGENT Craft, and Slate Auto, with no direct public comparables available.

The stage-progression method is the only applicable triangulation approach given data constraints. This method applies a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical of Series C to next-stage progression against the $500M last post-money valuation, implying a forward value of $850M. Peer-median analysis is not supportable: while 4 transportation peers exist in the broader dataset, none satisfy the narrow filters required (same stage, adjacent stage, or similar valuation tier) to constitute a robust comparable set of 3+ datapoints, and relying on all-sector medians would introduce unacceptable estimation error.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $722M to $977M, centered on $850M. This range reflects a single-method triangulation with inherent width to account for stage-progression uncertainty. Key uncertainties include the absence of same-stage transportation comparables to validate the 1.7x multiplier applicability to Harbinger specifically, and limited visibility into the financial and operational metrics of available private peers. Further diligence on burn rate, unit economics, and path to profitability would strengthen confidence in any point estimate within this range.
